subject: An immigrant woman holding a baby at Pier 21, Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada, 1948
context_for_lesson: A 1948 photograph of an immigrant woman with her baby at Pier 21, Halifax — Canada's principal immigrant arrival port from 1928 to 1971. Pier 21 processed nearly one million immigrants, including approximately 100,000 displaced persons and refugees from Europe in the late 1940s, many of them Holocaust survivors. Judy Abrams arrived in Canada in 1949, one year after this photograph, almost certainly via Pier 21. While this is not Judy, the scene depicts the kind of arrival moment Judy and her family would have experienced — a Jewish refugee family stepping onto Canadian soil after the war.
publisher_or_host: Wikimedia Commons (originally from Nova Scotia Archives, accession no. 1975-305)
author: E.A. Bollinger
license_text_verbatim: This Canadian work is in the public domain in Canada because its copyright has expired due to one of the following: 1. it was subject to Crown copyright and was first published more than 50 years ago, or it was not subject to Crown copyright, and 2. it is a photograph that was created prior to January 1, 1949.
license_summary: Public Domain in Canada and the United States. Free to use for any purpose including commercial, with no attribution required (though attribution is appreciated and given here).
attribution_required: Photo: E.A. Bollinger, Nova Scotia Archives, accession no. 1975-305 (Public Domain in Canada)
lesson_use_intent: Page 7 (journey-map page) — Montreal-1949 waypoint or 'Arrival in Canada' panel. Caption MUST clarify: 'Pier 21, Halifax, 1948. The arrival hall where almost one million immigrants and refugees entered Canada between 1928 and 1971. Judy Abrams arrived in Canada in 1949, likely through this same port. (This is not Judy.)' Do NOT imply this is Judy or her family.
concerns: Caption must clarify this is not Judy — it is general historical context for Canadian Jewish refugee arrival. Pier 21 is now the Canadian Museum of Immigration; the photograph is in the museum's source collection at Nova Scotia Archives.
